covered_sections: 3.5. Animal and plant cells>3.5. Animal and plant cells

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is a cell?

options.A: A multicellular organism with specialized tissues.
options.B: The smallest and basic unit of all living things.
options.C: A type of organelle that produces energy.
options.D: The genetic material found in the nucleus.

questions.hint: Remember the most fundamental level of biological organization.
questions.explanation: A cell is defined as the smallest and basic unit of living things, forming the foundation of all organisms. Option A describes a whole organism, not a single cell, while C and D refer to specific parts within a cell.

source_trace.content_anchor: Cell is the smallest and the basic unit of living things.

explanations_tiered.definition: A cell is the smallest structural and functional unit of all living organisms.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cells are the building blocks of life. Every living thing, from bacteria to whales, is composed of cells that carry out all necessary life processes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of cells like tiny Lego bricks. Just as bricks build a house, cells build every living thing.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: A cell is the smallest unit that can perform all life functions, such as growth and energy use. All organisms are composed of one or more cells.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The cell theory establishes the cell as the fundamental unit of life. Cellular biology investigates how these autonomous units interact to form complex multicellular systems with emergent properties.
reasoning_steps: Recall that all living things are composed of cells.
Identify the option that defines the basic structural unit.
Eliminate options that refer to specific cellular components or higher-level structures.
misconceptions: Some believe cells are only found in animals, but cells exist in all living things including plants and bacteria.
Cells are sometimes confused with organelles like mitochondria, but organelles are inside cells.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cell: smallest and basic unit of all living things.

glossary_entry.definition: The smallest structural and functional unit of all living organisms.

flashcard.front: What is the smallest unit of life?
flashcard.back: The cell is the smallest and basic unit of all living things.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the most basic unit of life?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The cell is the smallest and fundamental unit of all living organisms.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The smallest structural and functional unit of all living organisms, capable of carrying out life processes.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common confusion: students may think cells are only animal-related. Emphasize that plants, fungi, and bacteria also consist of cells.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The cell is the smallest and basic unit of living things, forming all organisms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: If a new organism is discovered and scientists determine it is composed of many cells with specialized functions, which principle of cell theory does this directly support?

options.A: Cells arise only from pre-existing cells.
options.B: All living organisms are composed of one or more cells.
options.C: Cells are the smallest unit of life.
options.D: All cells contain a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.

questions.hint: Consider whether the organism fits the definition of being built from cells.
questions.explanation: The principle that all living organisms are composed of one or more cells is supported by finding that a new organism is made of many cells. Option C is true but not directly illustrated by multicellularity, while D describes only eukaryotic cells.

source_trace.content_anchor: Living organisms are composed of one or more cells.

explanations_tiered.definition: Cell theory states that all living things are composed of cells, cells are the basic unit of life, and new cells arise from existing cells.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cell theory is a foundational biological principle. It establishes that cells are the universal building blocks of all organisms and that life continues through cell division.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a big apartment building made of many small rooms. The building is like a multicellular organism, and the rooms are the cells.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Cell theory includes three main ideas: all organisms consist of cells, the cell is the smallest unit of life, and all cells come from pre-existing cells.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The cell theory unified biology by establishing that all life shares a common structural unit. Modern research extends this to show how cellular specialization enables complex functions in multicellular organisms.
reasoning_steps: Identify the key fact in the question: the organism is multicellular.
Recall the three principles of cell theory.
Match the fact about multicellularity to the principle about organisms being composed of cells.
misconceptions: Some think cell theory applies only to animals, but it applies to all living things including plants and bacteria.
Cell theory does not state that all cells have a nucleus; prokaryotes lack a nucleus.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cell theory: all life made of cells; cells are basic unit; cells come from cells.

glossary_entry.definition: The biological principle stating that all living organisms are composed of one or more cells, the cell is the basic unit of life, and new cells arise from existing cells.

flashcard.front: What are the three main principles of cell theory?
flashcard.back: 1. All organisms are made of cells. 2. The cell is the smallest unit of life. 3. Cells arise only from pre-existing cells.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What evidence supports that all organisms are made of cells?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Observing any living thing under a microscope reveals cellular structure, from single bacteria to complex animals.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A fundamental biological principle describing cells as the basic unit of life.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Cell theory: 1) Cells make organisms. 2) Cells are basic unit. 3) Cells come from cells.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this question to check if students can apply cell theory principles to novel scenarios rather than just recite them.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Outline the contributions to cell theory
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The discovery of a multicellular organism directly supports the principle that all living things are composed of one or more cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which organelle contains the DNA and controls all activities of a eukaryotic cell?
questions.hint: Think about the 'control center' of the cell.
questions.explanation: The nucleus contains DNA and acts as the control center, directing cell activities including division and protein synthesis. Mitochondria produce energy, ribosomes make proteins, and the Golgi apparatus packages materials.

source_trace.content_anchor: The nucleus determines what the cell will be and controls cell division.

explanations_tiered.definition: The nucleus is a double-membrane-bound organelle that contains DNA and regulates all cellular activities.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The nucleus is the largest organelle in eukaryotic cells. It houses the genetic material and coordinates cell functions like growth, metabolism, and reproduction.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The nucleus is like the brain of the cell. Just as your brain controls your body, the nucleus controls the cell.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The nucleus contains chromosomes made of DNA. It determines the cell's type and role, such as becoming a muscle or nerve cell, and controls when the cell divides.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The nuclear envelope separates transcription (in the nucleus) from translation (in the cytoplasm). The nucleolus within the nucleus assembles ribosomes, which then exit through nuclear pores.
reasoning_steps: Identify which organelle stores genetic information (DNA).
Recall that DNA controls cell activities by directing protein production.
Select the organelle known as the control center of the cell.
misconceptions: Some think all cells have a nucleus, but prokaryotic cells lack a nucleus.
The nucleolus is sometimes mistaken for a separate organelle; it is a region inside the nucleus.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Nucleus: contains DNA, controls cell activities and division.

glossary_entry.definition: The membrane-bound organelle in eukaryotic cells that contains DNA and controls cellular activities.

flashcard.front: Which organelle is the control center of a eukaryotic cell?
flashcard.back: The nucleus contains DNA and controls all cell activities.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does the nucleus contain and control?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The nucleus contains DNA and controls all cellular activities and division.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The membrane-bound organelle containing DNA that acts as the control center of eukaryotic cells.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that the nucleus is only found in eukaryotic cells, not prokaryotes.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify the main structures of a cell
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The nucleus contains DNA and controls all cell activities. Mitochondria produce energy; ribosomes synthesize proteins; Golgi packages materials.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the primary function of the cell membrane?

options.A: To produce energy for the cell
options.B: To control what enters and leaves the cell

questions.hint: Consider how the cell maintains its internal environment.
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable and controls the movement of substances such as oxygen, water, and wastes into and out of the cell. Energy production occurs in mitochondria, protein synthesis on ribosomes, and DNA storage in the nucleus.

source_trace.content_anchor: Cell membrane controls what enters into and leaves a cell.

explanations_tiered.definition: The cell membrane is a selectively permeable barrier that regulates the passage of substances into and out of the cell.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: All cells have a cell membrane that separates the interior from the external environment. Its selective permeability allows essential nutrients to enter while keeping out harmful materials and expelling wastes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The cell membrane acts like a security guard at a gate, deciding who can enter and leave the cell.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The cell membrane controls transport through passive processes like diffusion and osmosis, as well as active transport using energy. This maintains homeostasis.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The fluid mosaic model describes the cell membrane as a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins. Channel proteins and carrier proteins facilitate selective transport, while the hydrophobic core blocks polar molecules.
reasoning_steps: Recall that the cell must exchange materials with its environment.
Identify which structure directly regulates this exchange.
Eliminate options describing functions of other organelles.
misconceptions: Some think the cell membrane is solid and rigid, but it is fluid and flexible.
The cell wall in plants is sometimes confused with the cell membrane, but the membrane is inside the wall.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Cell membrane: controls entry/exit of substances.

glossary_entry.definition: A selectively permeable barrier that surrounds the cell and regulates transport of materials into and out of the cell.

flashcard.front: What is the main job of the cell membrane?
flashcard.back: The cell membrane controls what enters and leaves the cell.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does the cell membrane regulate its internal environment?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: It is selectively permeable and controls what enters and leaves the cell.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The selectively permeable outer covering that separates the cell's interior from its environment.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Highlight the term 'selectively permeable' and contrast with the cell wall's rigid role in plants.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the function of cell structures
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. The cell membrane controls transport. Mitochondria produce energy (A), ribosomes synthesize proteins (C), and the nucleus stores genetic material (D).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the function of ribosomes in a cell?
questions.hint: Ribosomes are often called the 'protein factories'.
questions.explanation: Ribosomes synthesize proteins by linking amino acids together according to instructions from mRNA. Mitochondria produce ATP, vacuoles store water, and lysosomes digest bacteria.

source_trace.content_anchor: The function of ribosomes is to synthesize proteins.

explanations_tiered.definition: Ribosomes are small cellular structures that synthesize proteins by linking amino acids.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Ribosomes are found in all cells, either free in the cytoplasm or attached to the rough endoplasmic reticulum. They translate genetic information into functional proteins.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Ribosomes are like tiny factories that build proteins, which are the building blocks and workers of the cell.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Ribosomes read mRNA sequences and assemble amino acids into polypeptide chains, which fold into functional proteins. This process is called translation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Ribosomes are composed of rRNA and protein subunits. They catalyze peptide bond formation via peptidyl transferase activity. Prokaryotic ribosomes (70S) differ from eukaryotic ribosomes (80S), a target for certain antibiotics.
reasoning_steps: Recall the main function of ribosomes in protein production.
Differentiate ribosomes from other organelles with different roles.
Select the option that matches protein synthesis.
misconceptions: Some think ribosomes are only found in animal cells, but all cells have ribosomes.
Ribosomes are sometimes confused with the endoplasmic reticulum; ribosomes are small dots, while ER is a membrane system.

glossary_entry.definition: A cellular structure that synthesizes proteins by linking amino acids together.

flashcard.back: Ribosomes synthesize proteins for the cell.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What cellular structure is responsible for making proteins?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Ribosomes synthesize proteins by linking amino acids.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A small cellular structure responsible for protein synthesis.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Point out that ribosomes are not membrane-bound, which differs from most other organelles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify the main structures of a cell
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Ribosomes synthesize proteins. Mitochondria produce ATP (A), vacuoles store water (C), and lysosomes digest bacteria (D).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Why are mitochondria often called the 'powerhouses' of the cell?

options.A: They store large amounts of water.

questions.hint: Think about the role of energy production.
questions.explanation: Mitochondria generate ATP through cellular respiration, providing energy for cellular activities. Vacuoles store water, ribosomes synthesize proteins, and lysosomes contain digestive enzymes.

source_trace.content_anchor: Mitochondria are often described as the 'powerhouse' of the cell since they are the sites of respiration.

explanations_tiered.definition: Mitochondria are double-membrane organelles that convert chemical energy from food into ATP through cellular respiration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Mitochondria are found in nearly all eukaryotic cells. They have an inner membrane folded into cristae, which increases surface area for the electron transport chain and ATP synthesis.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Mitochondria are like batteries that charge the cell with energy so it can work and move.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Mitochondria carry out aerobic respiration, breaking down glucose to produce ATP. The folded inner membrane (cristae) provides space for the reactions that release energy.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Mitochondria contain their own DNA and ribosomes, supporting the endosymbiotic theory. The Krebs cycle occurs in the matrix, while oxidative phosphorylation on the cristae produces approximately 36 ATP per glucose molecule.
reasoning_steps: Recall the nickname 'powerhouse' and what it implies about function.
Identify which organelle produces usable energy for the cell.
Match the energy-producing organelle to ATP production.
misconceptions: Some think mitochondria are only in animal cells, but plant cells also have mitochondria.
Mitochondria are sometimes confused with chloroplasts, which perform photosynthesis.

glossary_entry.definition: Double-membrane organelles that produce ATP through cellular respiration.

flashcard.front: What is the function of mitochondria?
flashcard.back: Mitochondria produce ATP energy through cellular respiration.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which organelle is known as the powerhouse of the cell?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Mitochondria produce ATP energy through cellular respiration.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Organelles that generate ATP through cellular respiration; the 'powerhouses' of the cell.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Connect to energy demands of different cell types: muscle cells have many mitochondria.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the function of cell structures
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Mitochondria produce ATP through respiration. Vacuoles store water (A), ribosomes synthesize proteins (C), and lysosomes have digestive enzymes (D).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is the main function of chloroplasts in plant cells?

options.A: To perform photosynthesis and produce glucose
options.B: To store water and maintain cell shape

questions.hint: Recall which process uses chlorophyll to trap sunlight.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis, converting light energy into chemical energy in glucose. Vacuoles store water, lysosomes digest materials, and ribosomes synthesize proteins.

source_trace.content_anchor: Chloroplasts are the site for photosynthesis and contain chlorophyll that traps sunlight energy.

explanations_tiered.definition: Chloroplasts are double-membrane organelles in plant cells that perform photosynthesis using chlorophyll.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Chloroplasts are found in green plant tissues, especially leaves. They convert light energy into chemical energy (glucose) through the light-dependent and Calvin cycle reactions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Chloroplasts are like solar panels that catch sunlight and turn it into food for the plant.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, a green pigment that absorbs light energy. This energy drives photosynthesis, producing glucose and oxygen from carbon dioxide and water.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Photosynthesis occurs in two stages: light reactions in thylakoid membranes produce ATP and NADPH, while the Calvin cycle in the stroma fixes carbon dioxide into glucose. The overall equation is \(6CO_2 + 6H_2O + \text{light} \rightarrow C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6O_2\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that chloroplasts are unique to plant cells.
Connect chloroplasts to the process of photosynthesis.
Identify the product of photosynthesis (glucose).
misconceptions: Some think chloroplasts are found in all cells, but they are only in plant cells and some protists.
Mitochondria are sometimes confused with chloroplasts; chloroplasts make glucose, mitochondria break it down.

glossary_entry.definition: An organelle in plant cells that performs photosynthesis, converting light energy into chemical energy.

flashcard.back: Photosynthesis occurs in chloroplasts, producing glucose using sunlight.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the role of chloroplasts?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Chloroplasts perform photosynthesis, converting light energy into glucose.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The organelle in plant cells where photosynthesis occurs.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with mitochondria: chloroplasts build glucose, mitochondria break it down.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Describe the function of cell structures
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Chloroplasts perform photosynthesis. Vacuoles store water (B), lysosomes digest (C), ribosomes synthesize proteins (D).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What is a major structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?

options.A: Prokaryotes have a nucleus, while eukaryotes do not.
options.B: Prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles, including a nucleus.
options.C: Eukaryotes are always unicellular, while prokaryotes are multicellular.
options.D: Prokaryotes have chloroplasts, while eukaryotes do not.

questions.hint: Compare the presence of a nucleus and membrane-bound structures.
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells lack membrane-bound organelles and a nucleus, whereas eukaryotic cells have both. Option A reverses the correct relationship, C is false (both can be unicellular), and D is false (only some eukaryotes have chloroplasts).

source_trace.content_anchor: Prokaryotic cells don't have membrane-bound organelles or a nucleus. Eukaryotes have membrane-bound organelles and a nucleus.

explanations_tiered.definition: Prokaryotic cells are cells without a nucleus or membrane-bound organelles, while eukaryotic cells contain both.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: All cells are classified as either prokaryotic or eukaryotic. Prokaryotes (bacteria and archaea) are simpler and smaller, while eukaryotes (plants, animals, fungi, protists) have compartmentalized internal structures.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Prokaryotic cells are like a studio apartment: everything is in one open space. Eukaryotic cells are like a house with separate rooms (organelles).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Prokaryotic cells lack a nucleus; their DNA is in the nucleoid region. They also lack membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria or the ER. Eukaryotic cells have these structures.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The absence of internal membranes in prokaryotes means transcription and translation can occur simultaneously in the cytoplasm. Eukaryotes separate these processes with a nuclear envelope, allowing more complex regulation.
reasoning_steps: Recall the defining feature of prokaryotes: no nucleus.
Recall the defining feature of eukaryotes: nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
Identify the option that correctly states this difference.
misconceptions: Some think prokaryotes are a type of animal cell, but prokaryotes are bacteria and archaea.
Prokaryotes do have ribosomes, but they are not membrane-bound.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Prokaryote: no nucleus or membrane-bound organelles. Eukaryote: has both.

glossary_entry.definition: A cell that lacks a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.

flashcard.front: What is the key difference between prokaryotes and eukaryotes?
flashcard.back: Prokaryotes lack a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; eukaryotes have them.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Do prokaryotic cells have a nucleus?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No, prokaryotic cells lack a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A cell without a nucleus or other membrane-bound organelles.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Prokaryote: no nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this question to reinforce the fundamental classification of cell types.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Prokaryotes lack membrane-bound organelles and a nucleus. Eukaryotes have both, reversing option A. Both can be unicellular (C false). Only some eukaryotes have chloroplasts (D false).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: If a cell contains a nucleus, mitochondria, and an endoplasmic reticulum, into which category does it belong?
questions.hint: Consider which cell type has membrane-bound organelles.
questions.explanation: The presence of a nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria and ER is the defining feature of eukaryotic cells. Prokaryotes lack these structures, bacteria are prokaryotes, and viruses are not cells.

source_trace.content_anchor: Eukaryotes such as plants and animals cells have membrane-bound organelles and a nucleus.

explanations_tiered.definition: Eukaryotic cells are cells that contain a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Eukaryotes include animals, plants, fungi, and protists. Their internal membranes create specialized compartments (organelles) that separate different metabolic functions.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Eukaryotic cells are like a factory with different machines (organelles) in separate rooms, all controlled by a main office (the nucleus).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Eukaryotic cells have a true nucleus that stores DNA. They also have membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, and Golgi apparatus, each with specific functions.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The evolution of the nucleus allowed spatial separation of transcription and translation, enabling complex gene regulation. Endosymbiotic theory explains the origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts from captured prokaryotes.
reasoning_steps: Identify the structures listed: nucleus, mitochondria, ER.
Recall which cell type has a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
Conclude it is a eukaryotic cell.
misconceptions: Some think all eukaryotic cells have chloroplasts, but only plant cells do.
Eukaryotes are sometimes thought to always be multicellular, but many protists are unicellular eukaryotes.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Eukaryotic: has nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.

glossary_entry.definition: A cell that contains a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.

flashcard.front: What type of cell has a nucleus and mitochondria?
flashcard.back: A eukaryotic cell contains a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How can you identify a eukaryotic cell?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Eukaryotic cells have a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A cell with a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that 'true nucleus' (eu-karyon) means membrane-bound nucleus.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Differentiate between eukaryotic and prokaryotic cells
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Presence of a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles defines a eukaryotic cell. Prokaryotes and bacteria lack these; viruses are not cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which structure is present in plant cells but absent in animal cells?
questions.hint: Think about which organelle is involved in photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts are found only in plant cells (and some protists) and perform photosynthesis. Both plant and animal cells have mitochondria, cell membranes, and nuclei.

source_trace.content_anchor: Cell wall, chloroplast and large permanent vacuoles are found in plant cells while centriole is found only in animal cells.

explanations_tiered.definition: Chloroplasts are organelles found in plant cells that perform photosynthesis; they are absent in animal cells.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Plant cells have three structures not found in animal cells: cell wall, large central vacuole, and chloroplasts. Animal cells have centrioles that plant cells lack.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Plant cells have green chloroplasts to make food from sunlight, like tiny solar kitchens. Animal cells don't have these because animals eat food instead.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and capture light energy for photosynthesis. Plant cells need this for autotrophic nutrition, while animal cells are heterotrophic and obtain energy by consuming other organisms.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The endosymbiotic theory proposes chloroplasts originated from photosynthetic cyanobacteria engulfed by ancestral eukaryotes. This explains why chloroplasts have their own DNA and double membranes, and why only photosynthetic lineages retain them.
reasoning_steps: Recall structures unique to plant cells.
Recall structures shared by both cell types.
Identify which option is not shared and is unique to plants.
misconceptions: Some think plant cells do not have mitochondria, but they do for cellular respiration.
Chloroplasts are sometimes thought to be present in all plant parts, but they are mainly in leaves.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Plant cells have chloroplasts, cell wall, large vacuole; animal cells lack these.

glossary_entry.definition: An organelle found only in plant cells that performs photosynthesis.

flashcard.front: What organelle is found in plant cells but not animal cells?
flashcard.back: Chloroplasts are found in plant cells for photosynthesis, not in animal cells.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which organelle is unique to plant cells?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Chloroplasts are unique to plant cells for photosynthesis.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The plant cell organelle where photosynthesis occurs.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Plant cells: chloroplasts, cell wall, large vacuole.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use a Venn diagram to compare plant and animal cells, highlighting chloroplasts, cell wall, and centrioles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Compare and contrast plant and animal cells
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Chloroplasts are only in plant cells. Mitochondria, cell membrane, and nucleus are present in both plant and animal cells.
